# NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

Welcome to the official documentation for NautilusTrader!

NautilusTrader is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

The platform provides an extensive array of features and capabilities, coupled with open-ended flexibility for assembling trading systems using the framework. Given the breadth of information, and required pre-requisite knowledge, both beginners and experts alike may find the learning curve steep. However, this documentation aims to assist you in learning and understanding NautilusTrader, so that you can then leverage it to achieve your algorithmic trading goals.

If you have any questions or need further assistance, please reach out to the NautilusTrader community for support.

### Popular topics

#### Getting Started

Provides an overview, installation guide, and tutorials for setting up and running your first backtests.

[See Getting Started](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started)

#### Concepts

Understand key concepts, essential terminologies, core architecture, and algorithmic trading principles within NautilusTrader.

[See Concepts](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts)

#### Tutorials

Guided learning experience with a series of comprehensive step-by-step walkthroughs. Each tutorial targets specific features or workflows, enabling hands-on learning.

[See Tutorials](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials)

#### Integrations

Details adapter integrations for connecting with trading venues and data providers, unifying their raw APIs into a single interface.

[See Integrations](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations)

#### Developer Guide

These guides will help you add functionality to your trading operation and/or provide valuable contributions.

[See Developer Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/developer_guide)

note

The terms "NautilusTrader", "Nautilus" and "platform" are used interchageably throughout the documentation.

[](https://github.com/nautechsystems/nautilus_trader)
[](https://discord.gg/NautilusTrader)
[](https://www.linkedin.com/company/nautechsystems/)
[](https://twitter.com/NautilusTrader)

© 2026 Nautech Systems Pty Ltd. All rights reserved.

Welcome to the official documentation for NautilusTrader!

NautilusTrader is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

The platform provides an extensive array of features and capabilities, coupled with open-ended flexibility for assembling trading systems using the framework. Given the breadth of information, and required pre-requisite knowledge, both beginners and experts alike may find the learning curve steep. # Tutorials | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

On this page

The tutorials provide a guided learning experience with a series of comprehensive step-by-step walkthroughs. Each tutorial targets specific features or workflows, enabling hands-on learning. From basic tasks to more advanced operations, these tutorials cater to a wide range of skill levels.

info

Each tutorial is generated from a Jupyter notebook located in the docs [tutorials directory](https://github.com/nautechsystems/nautilus_trader/tree/develop/docs/tutorials)
. These notebooks serve as valuable learning aids and let you execute the code interactively.

tip

*   Make sure you are using the tutorial docs that match your NautilusTrader version:
*   **Latest**: These docs are built from the HEAD of the `master` branch and work with the latest stable release. See [https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/)
    .
*   **Nightly**: These docs are built from the HEAD of the `nightly` branch and work with bleeding-edge and experimental features. See [https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/)
    .

Running in docker[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/#running-in-docker "Direct link to Running in docker")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Alternatively, a self-contained dockerized Jupyter notebook server is available for download, which requires no setup or installation. This is the fastest way to get up and running to try out NautilusTrader. Note that deleting the container will also delete any data.

*   To get started, install docker:
    *   Go to [Docker installation guide](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/)
         and follow the instructions.
*   From a terminal, download the latest image:
    *   `docker pull ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly --platform linux/amd64`
*   Run the docker container, exposing the Jupyter port:
    *   `docker run -p 8888:8888 ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly`
*   When the container starts, a URL with an access token will be printed in the terminal. Copy that URL and open it in your browser, for example:
    *   [http://localhost:8888](http://localhost:8888/)
        

info

NautilusTrader currently exceeds the rate limit for Jupyter notebook logging (stdout output), therefore we set `log_level` to `ERROR` in the examples. Lowering this level to see more logging will cause the notebook to hang during cell execution. We are currently investigating a fix that involves either raising the configured rate limits for Jupyter, or throttling the log flushing from Nautilus.

*   [https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845)
    
*   [https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output](https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output)
    

*   [Running in docker](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/#running-in-docker)
    

[](https://github.com/nautechsystems/nautilus_trader)
[](https://discord.gg/NautilusTrader)
[](https://www.linkedin.com/company/nautechsystems/)
[](https://twitter.com/NautilusTrader)

# Getting Started | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

On this page

To get started with NautilusTrader, you will need:

*   A Python 3.12–3.14 environment with the `nautilus_trader` package installed.
*   A way to run Python scripts or Jupyter notebooks for backtesting and/or live trading.

[Installation](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/installation)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#installation "Direct link to installation")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Installation** guide will help to ensure that NautilusTrader is properly installed on your machine.

[Quickstart](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/quickstart)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#quickstart "Direct link to quickstart")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Quickstart** provides a step-by-step walk through for setting up your first backtest.

Examples in repository[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#examples-in-repository "Direct link to Examples in repository")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [online documentation](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/)
 shows just a subset of examples. For the full set, see this repository on GitHub.

The following table lists example locations ordered by recommended learning progression:

| Directory | Contains |
| --- | --- |
| [examples/](https://github.com/nautechsystems/nautilus_trader/tree/develop/examples) | Fully runnable, self-contained Python examples. |
| [docs/tutorials/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/) | Jupyter notebook tutorials demonstrating common workflows. |
| [docs/concepts/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/) | Concept guides with concise code snippets illustrating key features. |
| [nautilus\_trader/examples/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nautilus_trader/examples/) | Pure-Python examples of basic strategies, indicators, and execution algorithms. |
| [tests/unit\_tests/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/tests/unit_tests/) | Unit tests covering core functionality and edge cases. |

Backtesting API levels[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#backtesting-api-levels "Direct link to Backtesting API levels")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NautilusTrader provides two different API levels for backtesting:

| API Level | Description | Characteristics |
| --- | --- | --- |
| High-Level API | Uses `BacktestNode` and `TradingNode` | Recommended for production: easier transition to live trading; requires a Parquet-based data catalog. |
| Low-Level API | Uses `BacktestEngine` | Intended for library development: no live-trading path; direct component access; may encourage non–live-compatible patterns. |

warning

**One node per process**

Running multiple `BacktestNode` or `TradingNode` instances concurrently in the same process is not supported due to global singleton state. Sequential execution with proper disposal between runs is supported.

See [Processes and threads](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/architecture#processes-and-threads)
 for details.

Backtesting involves running simulated trading systems on historical data.

To get started backtesting with NautilusTrader you need to first understand the two different API levels which are provided, and which one may be more suitable for your intended use case.

info

For more information on which API level to choose, refer to the [Backtesting](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/backtesting)
 guide.

### [Backtest (low-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/backtest_low_level)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#backtest-low-level-api "Direct link to backtest-low-level-api")

This tutorial runs through how to load raw data (external to Nautilus) using data loaders and wranglers, and then use this data with a `BacktestEngine` to run a single backtest.

### [Backtest (high-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/backtest_high_level)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#backtest-high-level-api "Direct link to backtest-high-level-api")

This tutorial runs through how to load raw data (external to Nautilus) into the data catalog, and then use this data with a `BacktestNode` to run a single backtest.

Running in docker[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#running-in-docker "Direct link to Running in docker")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Alternatively, you can download a self-contained dockerized Jupyter notebook server, which requires no setup or installation. This is the fastest way to get up and running to try out NautilusTrader. Note that deleting the container will also delete any data.

*   To get started, install docker:
    *   Go to [Docker installation guide](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/)
         and follow the instructions.
*   From a terminal, download the latest image:
    *   `docker pull ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly --platform linux/amd64`
*   Run the docker container, exposing the jupyter port:
    *   `docker run -p 8888:8888 ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly`
*   Open your web browser to `localhost:{port}`:
    *   [http://localhost:8888](http://localhost:8888/)
        

info

NautilusTrader currently exceeds the rate limit for Jupyter notebook logging (stdout output), therefore we set `log_level` to `ERROR` in the examples. Lowering this level to see more logging will cause the notebook to hang during cell execution. We are currently investigating a fix that involves either raising the configured rate limits for Jupyter, or throttling the log flushing from Nautilus.

*   [https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845)
    
*   [https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output](https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output)
    

*   [Installation](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#installation)
    
*   [Quickstart](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#quickstart)
    
*   [Examples in repository](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#examples-in-repository)
    
*   [Backtesting API levels](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#backtesting-api-levels)
    *   [Backtest (low-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#backtest-low-level-api)
        
    *   [Backtest (high-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#backtest-high-level-api)
        
*   [Running in docker](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started/#running-in-docker)

# Concepts | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

On this page

Concept guides introduce and explain the foundational ideas, components, and best practices that underpin the NautilusTrader platform. These guides are designed to provide both conceptual and practical insights, helping you navigate the system's architecture, strategies, data management, execution flow, and more. Explore the following guides to deepen your understanding and make the most of NautilusTrader.

[Overview](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/overview)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#overview "Direct link to overview")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Overview** guide covers the main features and intended use cases for the platform.

[Architecture](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/architecture)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#architecture "Direct link to architecture")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Architecture** guide dives deep into the foundational principles, structures, and designs that underpin the platform. It is ideal for developers, system architects, or anyone curious about the inner workings of NautilusTrader.

[Actors](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/actors)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#actors "Direct link to actors")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `Actor` serves as the foundational component for interacting with the trading system. The **Actors** guide covers capabilities and implementation specifics.

[Strategies](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/strategies)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#strategies "Direct link to strategies")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `Strategy` is at the heart of the NautilusTrader user experience when writing and working with trading strategies. The **Strategies** guide covers how to implement strategies for the platform.

[Instruments](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/instruments)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#instruments "Direct link to instruments")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Instruments** guide covers the different instrument definition specifications for tradable assets and contracts.

[Data](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/data)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#data "Direct link to data")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The NautilusTrader platform defines a range of built-in data types crafted specifically to represent a trading domain. The **Data** guide covers working with both built-in and custom data.

[Execution](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/execution)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#execution "Direct link to execution")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NautilusTrader can handle trade execution and order management for multiple strategies and venues simultaneously (per instance). The **Execution** guide covers components involved in execution, as well as the flow of execution messages (commands and events).

[Orders](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/orders)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#orders "Direct link to orders")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Orders** guide provides more details about the available order types for the platform, along with the execution instructions supported for each. Advanced order types and emulated orders are also covered.

[Positions](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/positions)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#positions "Direct link to positions")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Positions** guide explains how positions work in NautilusTrader, including their lifecycle, aggregation from order fills, profit and loss calculations, and the important concept of position snapshotting for netting OMS configurations.

[Cache](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/cache)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#cache "Direct link to cache")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `Cache` is a central in-memory data store for managing all trading-related data. The **Cache** guide covers capabilities and best practices of the cache.

[Message Bus](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/message_bus)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#message-bus "Direct link to message-bus")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `MessageBus` is the core communication system enabling decoupled messaging patterns between components, including point-to-point, publish/subscribe, and request/response. The **Message Bus** guide covers capabilities and best practices of the `MessageBus`.

[Portfolio](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/portfolio)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#portfolio "Direct link to portfolio")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `Portfolio` serves as the central hub for managing and tracking all positions across active strategies for the trading node or backtest. It consolidates position data from multiple instruments, providing a unified view of your holdings, risk exposure, and overall performance. Explore this section to understand how NautilusTrader aggregates and updates portfolio state to support effective trading and risk management.

[Reports](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/reports)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#reports "Direct link to reports")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Reports** guide covers the reporting capabilities in NautilusTrader, including execution reports, portfolio analysis reports, PnL accounting considerations, and how reports are used for backtest post-run analysis.

[Logging](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/logging)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#logging "Direct link to logging")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The platform provides logging for both backtesting and live trading using a high-performance logger implemented in Rust.

[Backtesting](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/backtesting)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#backtesting "Direct link to backtesting")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Backtesting with NautilusTrader is a methodical simulation process that replicates trading activities using a specific system implementation.

[Visualization](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/visualization)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#visualization "Direct link to visualization")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Visualization** guide covers the interactive tearsheet system for analyzing backtest results, including available charts, themes, customization options, and how to create custom visualizations using the extensible chart registry.

[Live Trading](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/live)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#live-trading "Direct link to live-trading")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Live trading in NautilusTrader enables traders to deploy their backtested strategies in real-time without code changes. This transition aims to maintain consistency, though there are key differences between backtesting and live trading.

[Adapters](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/adapters)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts/#adapters "Direct link to adapters")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The NautilusTrader design allows for integrating data providers and/or trading venues through adapter implementations. The **Adapters** guide covers requirements and best practices for developing new integration adapters for the platform.

note

The [API Reference](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/api_reference/)
 documentation should be considered the source of truth for the platform. If there are any discrepancies between concepts described here and the API Reference, then the API Reference should be considered the correct information. We are working to ensure that concepts stay up-to-date with the API Reference and will be introducing doc tests in the near future to help with this.

# Developer Guide | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/developer_guide/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

On this page

Welcome to the developer guide for NautilusTrader!

Here you'll find guidance on developing and extending NautilusTrader to meet your trading needs or to contribute improvements back to the project.

info

This guide is structured so that automated tooling can consume it alongside human readers.

We believe in using the right tool for the job. The overall design philosophy is to fully utilize the high-level power of Python, with its rich ecosystem of frameworks and libraries, whilst leveraging Rust for performance-critical components and comprehensive type safety.

NautilusTrader uses a **Rust core with Python bindings** architecture:

*   **Rust** handles networking, data parsing, order matching, and other performance-critical operations.
*   **Python** provides the user-facing API for strategy development, configuration, and system integration.
*   **PyO3** bridges the two, exposing Rust functionality to Python with minimal overhead.

This approach combines Python's simplicity and ecosystem with Rust's performance and memory safety.

Contents[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/developer_guide/#contents "Direct link to Contents")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Integrations | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

On this page

NautilusTrader uses modular _adapters_ to connect to trading venues and data providers, translating raw APIs into a unified interface and normalized domain model.

The following integrations are currently supported:

| Name | ID  | Type | Status | Docs |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Betfair](https://betfair.com/) | `BETFAIR` | Sports Betting Exchange | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/betfair) |
| [Binance](https://binance.com/) | `BINANCE` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/binance) |
| [BitMEX](https://www.bitmex.com/) | `BITMEX` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/bitmex) |
| [Bybit](https://www.bybit.com/) | `BYBIT` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/bybit) |
| [Coinbase International](https://www.coinbase.com/en/international-exchange) | `COINBASE_INTX` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/coinbase_intx) |
| [Databento](https://databento.com/) | `DATABENTO` | Data Provider | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/databento) |
| [Deribit](https://www.deribit.com/) | `DERIBIT` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/building-orange) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/deribit) |
| [dYdX v3](https://dydx.exchange/) | `DYDX` | Crypto Exchange (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/dydx) |
| [dYdX v4](https://dydx.exchange/) | `DYDX` | Crypto Exchange (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/building-orange) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/dydx) |
| [Hyperliquid](https://hyperliquid.xyz/) | `HYPERLIQUID` | Crypto Exchange (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/building-orange) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/hyperliquid) |
| [Interactive Brokers](https://www.interactivebrokers.com/) | `INTERACTIVE_BROKERS` | Brokerage (multi-venue) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/ib) |
| [Kraken](https://kraken.com/) | `KRAKEN` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/beta-yellow) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/kraken) |
| [OKX](https://okx.com/) | `OKX` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/okx) |
| [Polymarket](https://polymarket.com/) | `POLYMARKET` | Prediction Market (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/polymarket) |
| [Tardis](https://tardis.dev/) | `TARDIS` | Crypto Data Provider | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/tardis) |

*   **ID**: The default client ID for the integrations adapter clients.
*   **Type**: The type of integration (often the venue type).

Status[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/#status "Direct link to Status")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `building`: Under construction and likely not in a usable state.
*   `beta`: Completed to a minimally working state and in a 'beta' testing phase.
*   `stable`: Stabilized feature set and API, the integration has been tested by both developers and users to a reasonable level (some bugs may still remain).

Implementation goals[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/#implementation-goals "Direct link to Implementation goals")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The primary goal of NautilusTrader is to provide a unified trading system for use with a variety of integrations. To support the widest range of trading strategies, priority will be given to _standard_ functionality:

*   Requesting historical market data.
*   Streaming live market data.
*   Reconciling execution state.
*   Submitting standard order types with standard execution instructions.
*   Modifying existing orders (if possible on an exchange).
*   Canceling orders.

The implementation of each integration aims to meet the following criteria:

*   Low-level client components should match the exchange API as closely as possible.
*   The full range of an exchange's functionality (where applicable to NautilusTrader) should _eventually_ be supported.
*   Exchange specific data types will be added to support the functionality and return types which are reasonably expected by a user.
*   Actions unsupported by an exchange or NautilusTrader will be logged as a warning or error when invoked.

API unification[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/#api-unification "Direct link to API unification")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

All integrations must conform to NautilusTrader’s system API, requiring normalization and standardization:

*   Symbols should use the venue’s native symbol format unless disambiguation is required (e.g., Binance Spot vs. Binance Futures).
*   Timestamps must use UNIX epoch nanoseconds. If milliseconds are used, field/property names should explicitly end with `_ms`.

*   [Status](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/#status)
    
*   [Implementation goals](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/#implementation-goals)
    
*   [API unification](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations/#api-unification)

# Python API | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/api_reference/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

On this page

Welcome to the Python API reference for NautilusTrader!

The API reference provides detailed technical documentation for the NautilusTrader framework, including its modules, classes, methods, and functions. The reference is automatically generated from the latest NautilusTrader source code using [Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/)
.

Please note that there are separate references for different versions of NautilusTrader:

*   **Latest**: This API reference is built from the head of the `develop` branch and represents the latest stable release.
*   **Nightly**: This API reference is built from the head of the `nightly` branch and represents bleeding edge and experimental changes/features currently in development.

You can select the desired API reference from the **Versions** top left drop down menu.

Use the right navigation sidebar to explore the available modules and their contents. You can click on any item to view its detailed documentation, including parameter descriptions, and return value explanations.

Why Python?[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/api_reference/#why-python "Direct link to Why Python?")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Python was originally created decades ago as a simple scripting language with a clean straight forward syntax. It has since evolved into a fully fledged general purpose object-oriented programming language. Based on the TIOBE index, Python is currently the most popular programming language in the world. Not only that, Python has become the _de facto lingua franca_ of data science, machine learning, and artificial intelligence.

*   [Why Python?](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/api_reference/#why-python)

# NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: latest

Welcome to the official documentation for NautilusTrader!

NautilusTrader is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

The platform provides an extensive array of features and capabilities, coupled with open-ended flexibility for assembling trading systems using the framework. Given the breadth of information, and required pre-requisite knowledge, both beginners and experts alike may find the learning curve steep. However, this documentation aims to assist you in learning and understanding NautilusTrader, so that you can then leverage it to achieve your algorithmic trading goals.

If you have any questions or need further assistance, please reach out to the NautilusTrader community for support.

### Popular topics

#### Getting Started

Provides an overview, installation guide, and tutorials for setting up and running your first backtests.

[See Getting Started](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/getting_started)

#### Concepts

Understand key concepts, essential terminologies, core architecture, and algorithmic trading principles within NautilusTrader.

[See Concepts](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/concepts)

#### Tutorials

Guided learning experience with a series of comprehensive step-by-step walkthroughs. Each tutorial targets specific features or workflows, enabling hands-on learning.

[See Tutorials](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials)

#### Integrations

Details adapter integrations for connecting with trading venues and data providers, unifying their raw APIs into a single interface.

[See Integrations](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/integrations)

#### Developer Guide

These guides will help you add functionality to your trading operation and/or provide valuable contributions.

[See Developer Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/developer_guide)

note

The terms "NautilusTrader", "Nautilus" and "platform" are used interchageably throughout the documentation.

# Getting Started | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: nightly

On this page

To get started with NautilusTrader, you will need:

*   A Python 3.12–3.14 environment with the `nautilus_trader` package installed.
*   A way to run Python scripts or Jupyter notebooks for backtesting and/or live trading.

[Installation](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/installation)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#installation "Direct link to installation")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Installation** guide will help to ensure that NautilusTrader is properly installed on your machine.

[Quickstart](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/quickstart)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#quickstart "Direct link to quickstart")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Quickstart** provides a step-by-step walk through for setting up your first backtest.

Examples in repository[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#examples-in-repository "Direct link to Examples in repository")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [online documentation](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/)
 shows just a subset of examples. For the full set, see this repository on GitHub.

The following table lists example locations ordered by recommended learning progression:

| Directory | Contains |
| --- | --- |
| [examples/](https://github.com/nautechsystems/nautilus_trader/tree/develop/examples) | Fully runnable, self-contained Python examples. |
| [docs/tutorials/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/) | Jupyter notebook tutorials demonstrating common workflows. |
| [docs/concepts/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/concepts/) | Concept guides with concise code snippets illustrating key features. |
| [nautilus\_trader/examples/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nautilus_trader/examples/) | Pure-Python examples of basic strategies, indicators, and execution algorithms. |
| [tests/unit\_tests/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/tests/unit_tests/) | Unit tests covering core functionality and edge cases. |

Backtesting API levels[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#backtesting-api-levels "Direct link to Backtesting API levels")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NautilusTrader provides two different API levels for backtesting:

| API Level | Description | Characteristics |
| --- | --- | --- |
| High-Level API | Uses `BacktestNode` and `TradingNode` | Recommended for production: easier transition to live trading; requires a Parquet-based data catalog. |
| Low-Level API | Uses `BacktestEngine` | Intended for library development: no live-trading path; direct component access; may encourage non–live-compatible patterns. |

warning

**One node per process**

Running multiple `BacktestNode` or `TradingNode` instances concurrently in the same process is not supported due to global singleton state. Sequential execution with proper disposal between runs is supported.

See [Processes and threads](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/concepts/architecture#processes-and-threads)
 for details.

Backtesting involves running simulated trading systems on historical data.

To get started backtesting with NautilusTrader you need to first understand the two different API levels which are provided, and which one may be more suitable for your intended use case.

info

For more information on which API level to choose, refer to the [Backtesting](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/concepts/backtesting)
 guide.

### [Backtest (low-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/backtest_low_level)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#backtest-low-level-api "Direct link to backtest-low-level-api")

This tutorial runs through how to load raw data (external to Nautilus) using data loaders and wranglers, and then use this data with a `BacktestEngine` to run a single backtest.

### [Backtest (high-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/backtest_high_level)
[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#backtest-high-level-api "Direct link to backtest-high-level-api")

This tutorial runs through how to load raw data (external to Nautilus) into the data catalog, and then use this data with a `BacktestNode` to run a single backtest.

Running in docker[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#running-in-docker "Direct link to Running in docker")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Alternatively, you can download a self-contained dockerized Jupyter notebook server, which requires no setup or installation. This is the fastest way to get up and running to try out NautilusTrader. Note that deleting the container will also delete any data.

*   To get started, install docker:
    *   Go to [Docker installation guide](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/)
         and follow the instructions.
*   From a terminal, download the latest image:
    *   `docker pull ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly --platform linux/amd64`
*   Run the docker container, exposing the jupyter port:
    *   `docker run -p 8888:8888 ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly`
*   Open your web browser to `localhost:{port}`:
    *   [http://localhost:8888](http://localhost:8888/)
        

info

NautilusTrader currently exceeds the rate limit for Jupyter notebook logging (stdout output), therefore we set `log_level` to `ERROR` in the examples. Lowering this level to see more logging will cause the notebook to hang during cell execution. We are currently investigating a fix that involves either raising the configured rate limits for Jupyter, or throttling the log flushing from Nautilus.

*   [https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845)
    
*   [https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output](https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output)
    

*   [Installation](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#installation)
    
*   [Quickstart](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#quickstart)
    
*   [Examples in repository](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#examples-in-repository)
    
*   [Backtesting API levels](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#backtesting-api-levels)
    *   [Backtest (low-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#backtest-low-level-api)
        
    *   [Backtest (high-level API)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#backtest-high-level-api)
        
*   [Running in docker](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/getting_started/#running-in-docker)

# nautilus_analysis - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_analysis](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/index.html#)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_analysis Copy item path
=======================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_analysis/lib.rs.html#16-70)

Expand description

Portfolio analysis and performance metrics for [NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
.

The `nautilus-analysis` crate provides a comprehensive suite of portfolio analysis tools and performance statistics for evaluating trading strategies and portfolios. This includes return-based metrics, PnL-based statistics, and risk measurements commonly used in quantitative finance:

*   Portfolio analyzer for tracking account states and positions.
*   Extensive collection of performance statistics and risk metrics.
*   Flexible statistic calculation framework supporting different data sources.
*   Support for multi-currency portfolios and unrealized PnL calculations.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/index.html#platform)
Platform
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical, trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case, i.e. whether to provide Python bindings for the [nautilus\_trader](https://pypi.org/project/nautilus_trader)
 Python package, or as part of a Rust only build.

*   `python`: Enables Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds the crate as a Python extension module.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/index.html#modules)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[analyzer](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/index.html "mod nautilus_analysis::analyzer")

[python](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/python/index.html "mod nautilus_analysis::python")

Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
.

[statistic](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/statistic/index.html "mod nautilus_analysis::statistic")

[statistics](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/statistics/index.html "mod nautilus_analysis::statistics")

Trading performance statistics and portfolio metrics.

Type Aliases[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/index.html#types)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Returns](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/type.Returns.html "type nautilus_analysis::Returns")

Type alias for time-indexed returns data used in portfolio analysis.

# Tutorials | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: nightly

On this page

The tutorials provide a guided learning experience with a series of comprehensive step-by-step walkthroughs. Each tutorial targets specific features or workflows, enabling hands-on learning. From basic tasks to more advanced operations, these tutorials cater to a wide range of skill levels.

info

Each tutorial is generated from a Jupyter notebook located in the docs [tutorials directory](https://github.com/nautechsystems/nautilus_trader/tree/develop/docs/tutorials)
. These notebooks serve as valuable learning aids and let you execute the code interactively.

tip

*   Make sure you are using the tutorial docs that match your NautilusTrader version:
*   **Latest**: These docs are built from the HEAD of the `master` branch and work with the latest stable release. See [https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/latest/tutorials/)
    .
*   **Nightly**: These docs are built from the HEAD of the `nightly` branch and work with bleeding-edge and experimental features. See [https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/)
    .

Running in docker[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/#running-in-docker "Direct link to Running in docker")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Alternatively, a self-contained dockerized Jupyter notebook server is available for download, which requires no setup or installation. This is the fastest way to get up and running to try out NautilusTrader. Note that deleting the container will also delete any data.

*   To get started, install docker:
    *   Go to [Docker installation guide](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/)
         and follow the instructions.
*   From a terminal, download the latest image:
    *   `docker pull ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly --platform linux/amd64`
*   Run the docker container, exposing the Jupyter port:
    *   `docker run -p 8888:8888 ghcr.io/nautechsystems/jupyterlab:nightly`
*   When the container starts, a URL with an access token will be printed in the terminal. Copy that URL and open it in your browser, for example:
    *   [http://localhost:8888](http://localhost:8888/)
        

info

NautilusTrader currently exceeds the rate limit for Jupyter notebook logging (stdout output), therefore we set `log_level` to `ERROR` in the examples. Lowering this level to see more logging will cause the notebook to hang during cell execution. We are currently investigating a fix that involves either raising the configured rate limits for Jupyter, or throttling the log flushing from Nautilus.

*   [https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/12845)
    
*   [https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output](https://github.com/deshaw/jupyterlab-limit-output)
    

*   [Running in docker](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/tutorials/#running-in-docker)

# nautilus_cli - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_cli](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_cli/index.html#)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_cli Copy item path
==================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_cli/lib.rs.html#16-77)

Expand description

Command-line interface and tools for [NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
.

The `nautilus-cli` crate provides a comprehensive command-line interface for managing and operating NautilusTrader installations. It includes tools for database management, system configuration, and operational utilities:

*   Database initialization and management commands.
*   PostgreSQL schema setup and maintenance.
*   Configuration validation and setup utilities.
*   System administration and operational tools.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_cli/index.html#platform)
Platform
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical, trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_cli/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case:

*   `defi`: Enables DeFi functionality including blockchain data access and pool analysis.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_cli/index.html#modules)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[opt](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_cli/opt/index.html "mod nautilus_cli::opt")

Functions[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_cli/index.html#functions)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[run](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_cli/fn.run.html "fn nautilus_cli::run")

Runs the Nautilus CLI based on the provided options.

# nautilus_backtest - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_backtest](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/index.html#)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_backtest Copy item path
=======================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_backtest/lib.rs.html#16-69)

Expand description

Backtest engine for [NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
.

The `nautilus-backtest` crate provides a comprehensive event-driven backtesting framework that allows quantitative traders to test and validate trading strategies on historical data with high fidelity market simulation. The system replicates real market conditions including:

*   Event-driven backtesting engine with simulated exchanges.
*   Market data replay with configurable latency and fill models.
*   Order matching engines with realistic execution simulation.
*   Multi-venue and multi-asset backtesting capabilities.
*   Comprehensive configuration and state management.
*   Integration with live trading systems for seamless deployment.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/index.html#platform)
Platform
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical, trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case, i.e. whether to provide Python bindings for the [nautilus\_trader](https://pypi.org/project/nautilus_trader)
 Python package, or as part of a Rust only build.

*   `ffi`: Enables the C foreign function interface (FFI) from [cbindgen](https://github.com/mozilla/cbindgen)
    .
*   `python`: Enables Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds the crate as a Python extension module.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/index.html#modules)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[accumulator](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/accumulator/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::accumulator")

[config](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/config/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::config")

[data\_client](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/data_client/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::data_client")

Provides a `BacktestDataClient` implementation for backtesting.

[data\_iterator](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/data_iterator/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::data_iterator")

[engine](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/engine/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::engine")

The core `BacktestEngine` for backtesting on historical data.

[exchange](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/exchange/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::exchange")

Provides a `SimulatedExchange` venue for backtesting on historical data.

[execution\_client](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/execution_client/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::execution_client")

Provides a `BacktestExecutionClient` implementation for backtesting.

[ffi](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/ffi/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::ffi")

C foreign function interface (FFI) from [cbindgen](https://github.com/mozilla/cbindgen)
.

[modules](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/modules/index.html "mod nautilus_backtest::modules")

# nautilus_binance - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_binance](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_binance/index.html#)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_binance Copy item path
======================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_binance/lib.rs.html#16-72)

Expand description

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 adapter for the [Binance](https://www.binance.com/)
 cryptocurrency exchange.

The `nautilus-binance` crate provides client bindings (HTTP & WebSocket), data models, and helper utilities that wrap the official **Binance API**, covering:

*   Spot trading (api.binance.com)
*   Spot margin trading
*   USD-M Futures (fapi.binance.com)
*   COIN-M Futures (dapi.binance.com)
*   European Options (eapi.binance.com)

The official Binance API reference can be found at [https://binance-docs.github.io/apidocs/](https://binance-docs.github.io/apidocs/)
.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_binance/index.html#platform)
Platform
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_binance/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case (Rust-only builds vs. Python bindings through PyO3).

*   `python`: Enables Python bindings via [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds as a Python extension module (used together with `python`).

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_binance/index.html#documentation)
Documentation
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

See [https://docs.rs/nautilus-binance](https://docs.rs/nautilus-binance)
 for the latest API documentation.

# Developer Guide | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: nightly

On this page

Welcome to the developer guide for NautilusTrader!

Here you'll find guidance on developing and extending NautilusTrader to meet your trading needs or to contribute improvements back to the project.

info

This guide is structured so that automated tooling can consume it alongside human readers.

We believe in using the right tool for the job. The overall design philosophy is to fully utilize the high-level power of Python, with its rich ecosystem of frameworks and libraries, whilst leveraging Rust for performance-critical components and comprehensive type safety.

NautilusTrader uses a **Rust core with Python bindings** architecture:

*   **Rust** handles networking, data parsing, order matching, and other performance-critical operations.
*   **Python** provides the user-facing API for strategy development, configuration, and system integration.
*   **PyO3** bridges the two, exposing Rust functionality to Python with minimal overhead.

This approach combines Python's simplicity and ecosystem with Rust's performance and memory safety.

Contents[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/#contents "Direct link to Contents")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [Environment Setup](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/environment_setup)

*   [Rust](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/rust)
    
*   [Python](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python)
    
*   [Testing](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing)
    
*   [Docs Style](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/docs)
    
*   [Release Notes](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/releases)
    
*   [Adapters](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/adapters)
    
*   [Benchmarking](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/benchmarking)
    
*   [FFI Memory Contract](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/ffi)
    

*   [Contents](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/#contents)

# Integrations | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: nightly

On this page

NautilusTrader uses modular _adapters_ to connect to trading venues and data providers, translating raw APIs into a unified interface and normalized domain model.

The following integrations are currently supported:

| Name | ID  | Type | Status | Docs |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [AX Exchange](https://architect.exchange/) | `AX` | Perpetuals Exchange | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/building-orange) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/architect_ax) |
| [Architect](https://architect.co/) | `ARCHITECT` | Brokerage (multi-venue) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/planned-gray) | \-  |
| [Betfair](https://betfair.com/) | `BETFAIR` | Sports Betting Exchange | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/betfair) |
| [Binance](https://binance.com/) | `BINANCE` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/binance) |
| [BitMEX](https://www.bitmex.com/) | `BITMEX` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/bitmex) |
| [Bybit](https://www.bybit.com/) | `BYBIT` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/bybit) |
| [Coinbase International](https://www.coinbase.com/en/international-exchange) | `COINBASE_INTX` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/coinbase_intx) |
| [Databento](https://databento.com/) | `DATABENTO` | Data Provider | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/databento) |
| [Deribit](https://www.deribit.com/) | `DERIBIT` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/beta-yellow) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/deribit) |
| [dYdX v3](https://dydx.exchange/) | `DYDX` | Crypto Exchange (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/dydx) |
| [dYdX v4](https://dydx.exchange/) | `DYDX` | Crypto Exchange (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/building-orange) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/dydx) |
| [Hyperliquid](https://hyperliquid.xyz/) | `HYPERLIQUID` | Crypto Exchange (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/building-orange) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/hyperliquid) |
| [Interactive Brokers](https://www.interactivebrokers.com/) | `INTERACTIVE_BROKERS` | Brokerage (multi-venue) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/ib) |
| [Kraken](https://kraken.com/) | `KRAKEN` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/beta-yellow) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/kraken) |
| [OKX](https://okx.com/) | `OKX` | Crypto Exchange (CEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/okx) |
| [Polymarket](https://polymarket.com/) | `POLYMARKET` | Prediction Market (DEX) | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/polymarket) |
| [Tardis](https://tardis.dev/) | `TARDIS` | Crypto Data Provider | ![status](https://img.shields.io/badge/stable-green) | [Guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/tardis) |

*   **ID**: The default client ID for the integrations adapter clients.
*   **Type**: The type of integration (often the venue type).

Status[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/#status "Direct link to Status")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `planned`: Planned for future development.
*   `building`: Under construction and likely not in a usable state.
*   `beta`: Completed to a minimally working state and in a 'beta' testing phase.
*   `stable`: Stabilized feature set and API, the integration has been tested by both developers and users to a reasonable level (some bugs may still remain).

Implementation goals[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/#implementation-goals "Direct link to Implementation goals")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The primary goal of NautilusTrader is to provide a unified trading system for use with a variety of integrations. To support the widest range of trading strategies, priority will be given to _standard_ functionality:

*   Requesting historical market data.
*   Streaming live market data.
*   Reconciling execution state.
*   Submitting standard order types with standard execution instructions.
*   Modifying existing orders (if possible on an exchange).
*   Canceling orders.

The implementation of each integration aims to meet the following criteria:

*   Low-level client components should match the exchange API as closely as possible.
*   The full range of an exchange's functionality (where applicable to NautilusTrader) should _eventually_ be supported.
*   Exchange specific data types will be added to support the functionality and return types which are reasonably expected by a user.
*   Actions unsupported by an exchange or NautilusTrader will be logged as a warning or error when invoked.

API unification[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/#api-unification "Direct link to API unification")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

All integrations must conform to NautilusTrader’s system API, requiring normalization and standardization:

*   Symbols should use the venue’s native symbol format unless disambiguation is required (e.g., Binance Spot vs. Binance Futures).
*   Timestamps must use UNIX epoch nanoseconds. If milliseconds are used, field/property names should explicitly end with `_ms`.

*   [Status](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/#status)
    
*   [Implementation goals](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/#implementation-goals)
    
*   [API unification](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/integrations/#api-unification)

[](https://discord.gg/NautilusTrader)
[](https://www.linkedin.com/company/nautechsystems/)
[](https://twitter.com/NautilusTrader)

© 2026 Nautech Systems Pty Ltd. All rights reserved.

# nautilus_blockchain - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_blockchain](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_blockchain Copy item path
=========================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_blockchain/lib.rs.html#16-80)

Expand description

Blockchain data adapter for [NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
.

The `nautilus-blockchain` crate provides a high-performance, universal, extensible adapter for ingesting DeFi data from decentralized exchanges (DEXs), liquidity pools, and on-chain events. It enables you to power analytics pipelines and trading strategies with real-time and historical on-chain data.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#platform)
Platform
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case, i.e. whether to provide Python bindings for the [nautilus\_trader](https://pypi.org/project/nautilus_trader)
 Python package, or as part of a Rust only build.

*   `hypersync`: Enables the [HyperSync](https://envio.dev/#hypersync)
     client integration.
*   `python`: Enables Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds as a Python extension module (used with `python`).
*   `turmoil`: Enables deterministic network simulation testing with [turmoil](https://github.com/tokio-rs/turmoil)
    .

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#modules)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Caching layer for blockchain entities and domain objects.

[constants](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/constants/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::constants")

[contracts](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/contracts/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::contracts")

Smart contract interfaces and utilities for blockchain interactions.

[data](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/data/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::data")

[decode](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/decode/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::decode")

Blockchain event data structures and parsers.

[exchanges](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/exchanges/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::exchanges")

[execution](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/execution/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::execution")

.

Expand description

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 adapter for the [Bybit](https://www.bybit.com/)
 cryptocurrency exchange.

The `nautilus-bybit` crate provides client bindings (HTTP & WebSocket), data models, and helper utilities that wrap the official **Bybit v5 API**.

The official Bybit API reference can be found at [https://bybit-exchange.github.io/docs/v5/intro](https://bybit-exchange.github.io/docs/v5/intro)
. All public links inside this crate reference the English version of the documentation.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/index.html#platform)
Platform
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case (Rust-only builds vs. Python bindings through PyO3).

*   `python`: Enables Python bindings via [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds as a Python extension module (used together with `python`).

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/index.html#documentation)
Documentation
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

See [https://docs.rs/nautilus-bybit](https://docs.rs/nautilus-bybit)
 for the latest API documentation.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/index.html#modules)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[common](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/common/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::common")

Shared primitives and utilities for the Bybit adapter.

[config](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/config/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::config")

Configuration structures for the Bybit adapter.

[data](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/data/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::data")

Data client implementation for the Bybit adapter.

[error](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/error/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::error")

Unified error handling for the Bybit adapter.

[execution](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/execution/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::execution")

Execution client implementation for the Bybit adapter.

[http](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/http/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::http")

HTTP client bindings for the Bybit adapter.

[python](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/python/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::python")

Python bindings from `pyo3`.

[websocket](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/websocket/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::websocket")

WebSocket client bindings for the Bybit adapter.

---

Expand description

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 adapter for [Coinbase International](https://www.coinbase.com/en/international-exchange)
 exchange.

The `nautilus-coinbase-intx` crate provides integration with the Coinbase International API for institutional trading on their derivatives exchange.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_coinbase_intx/index.html#platform)
Platform
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical, trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_coinbase_intx/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case, i.e. whether to provide Python bindings for the [nautilus\_trader](https://pypi.org/project/nautilus_trader)
 Python package, or as part of a Rust only build.

*   `python`: Enables Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds as a Python extension module (used with `python`).
