# nautilus_cli - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_cli](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cli/index.html#)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_cli Copy item path
==================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/src/nautilus_cli/lib.rs.html#16-77)

Expand description

Command-line interface and tools for [NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
.

The `nautilus-cli` crate provides a comprehensive command-line interface for managing and operating NautilusTrader installations. It includes tools for database management, system configuration, and operational utilities:

*   Database initialization and management commands.
*   PostgreSQL schema setup and maintenance.
*   Configuration validation and setup utilities.
*   System administration and operational tools.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cli/index.html#platform)
Platform
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical, trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cli/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case:

*   `defi`: Enables DeFi functionality including blockchain data access and pool analysis.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cli/index.html#modules)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Expand description

Unified error handling for the Bybit adapter.

This module provides a comprehensive error taxonomy that distinguishes between retryable, non-retryable, and fatal errors, with proper context preservation for debugging and operational monitoring.

Re-exports[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bybit/error/index.html#reexports)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# nautilus_bitmex::common - Rust

[Module common](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/common/index.html#)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[nautilus\_bitmex](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/index.html)

Module common Copy item path
============================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_bitmex/common/mod.rs.html#16-32)

Expand description

Common types and utilities shared across the BitMEX adapter.

This module provides reusable components that are used by both the HTTP and WebSocket clients, including:

*   Constants for BitMEX URLs and venue identifier.
*   Credential management for API authentication.
*   Enumerations for order types, sides, and statuses.
*   Parsing utilities for currency codes and other data transformations.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/common/index.html#modules)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[consts](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/common/consts/index.html "mod nautilus_bitmex::common::consts")

BitMEX adapter constants including base URLs and the venue identifier.

[credential](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/common/credential/index.html "mod nautilus_bitmex::common::credential")

API credential utilities for signing BitMEX requests.

[enums](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/common/enums/index.html "mod nautilus_bitmex::common::enums")

BitMEX-specific enumerations shared by HTTP and WebSocket components.

[parse](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/common/parse/index.html "mod nautilus_bitmex::common::parse")

Shared parsing helpers that transform BitMEX payloads into Nautilus types.

[urls](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/common/urls/index.html "mod nautilus_bitmex::common::urls")

URL helpers for BitMEX services.

# nautilus_cryptography - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_cryptography](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/index.html#)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_cryptography Copy item path
===========================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/src/nautilus_cryptography/lib.rs.html#16-62)

Expand description

Cryptographic utilities and security functions for [NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
.

The `nautilus-cryptography` crate provides essential cryptographic primitives and security utilities required for secure communication with trading venues and data providers. This includes digital signing, TLS configuration, and cryptographic provider management:

*   HMAC-based message authentication and signing.
*   Digital signatures using RSA and Ed25519 algorithms.
*   TLS client configuration with platform certificate verification.
*   Cryptographic provider management and initialization.
*   Secure encoding and decoding utilities.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/index.html#platform)
Platform
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical, trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case, i.e. whether to provide Python bindings for the [nautilus\_trader](https://pypi.org/project/nautilus_trader)
 Python package, or as part of a Rust only build.

*   `python`: Enables Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds the crate as a Python extension module.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/index.html#modules)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[providers](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/providers/index.html "mod nautilus_cryptography::providers")

[python](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/python/index.html "mod nautilus_cryptography::python")

Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
.

[signing](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/signing/index.html "mod nautilus_cryptography::signing")

[tls](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_cryptography/tls/index.html "mod nautilus_cryptography::tls")

[nautilus\_blockchain](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/index.html)

Module cache Copy item path
===========================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_blockchain/cache/mod.rs.html#16-863)

Expand description

Caching layer for blockchain entities and domain objects.

This module provides an in-memory cache with optional PostgreSQL persistence for storing and retrieving blockchain-related data such as blocks, tokens, pools, swaps, and other DeFi protocol events.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/cache/index.html#modules)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[copy](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/cache/copy/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::cache::copy")

PostgreSQL COPY BINARY operations for high-performance bulk data loading.

[rows](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/cache/rows/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::cache::rows")

[types](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_blockchain/cache/types/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::cache::types")

# nautilus_coinbase_intx - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_coinbase\_intx](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/index.html#)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_coinbase\_intx Copy item path
=============================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/src/nautilus_coinbase_intx/lib.rs.html#16-56)

Expand description

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 adapter for [Coinbase International](https://www.coinbase.com/en/international-exchange)
 exchange.

The `nautilus-coinbase-intx` crate provides integration with the Coinbase International API for institutional trading on their derivatives exchange.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/index.html#platform)
Platform
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical, trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case, i.e. whether to provide Python bindings for the [nautilus\_trader](https://pypi.org/project/nautilus_trader)
 Python package, or as part of a Rust only build.

*   `python`: Enables Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds as a Python extension module (used with `python`).

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/index.html#modules)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[common](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/common/index.html "mod nautilus_coinbase_intx::common")

[config](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/config/index.html "mod nautilus_coinbase_intx::config")

[fix](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/fix/index.html "mod nautilus_coinbase_intx::fix")

[http](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/http/index.html "mod nautilus_coinbase_intx::http")

[python](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/python/index.html "mod nautilus_coinbase_intx::python")

Python bindings from `pyo3`.

[websocket](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_coinbase_intx/websocket/index.html "mod nautilus_coinbase_intx::websocket")

# PortfolioStatistic in nautilus_analysis::statistic - Rust

[PortfolioStatistic](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/statistic/trait.PortfolioStatistic.html#)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[nautilus\_analysis](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/index.html)
::[statistic](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/statistic/index.html)

Trait PortfolioStatistic Copy item path
=======================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_analysis/statistic.rs.html#30-110)

    pub trait PortfolioStatistic: Debug {
        type Item;
    
        // Required method
        fn name(&self) -> String;
    
        // Provided methods
        fn calculate_from_returns(&self, returns: &Returns) -> Option<Self::Item> { ... }
        fn calculate_from_realized_pnls(
            &self,
            realized_pnls: &[f64],
        ) -> Option<Self::Item> { ... }
        fn calculate_from_orders(
            &self,
            orders: Vec<Box<dyn Order>>,
        ) -> Option<Self::Item> { ... }
        fn calculate_from_positions(
            &self,
            positions: &[Position],
        ) -> Option<Self::Item> { ... }
        fn check_valid_returns(&self, returns: &Returns) -> bool { ... }
        fn downsample_to_daily_bins(&self, returns: &Returns) -> Returns { ... }
        fn calculate_std(&self, returns: &Returns) -> f64 { ... }
    }

Expand description

Trait for portfolio performance statistics that can be calculated from different data sources.

This trait provides a flexible framework for implementing various financial performance metrics that can operate on returns, realized PnLs, orders, or positions data. Each statistic implementation should override the relevant calculation methods.

Required Associated Types[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/statistic/trait.PortfolioStatistic.html#required-associated-types)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_analysis/statistic.rs.html#31)

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_binance/error/enum.BinanceError.html#method.into_either)

#### fn [into\_either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)
(self, into\_left: [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left` is `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)

[Source](https://docs.rs/either/1/src/either/into_either.rs.html#55-57)
[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_binance/error/enum.BinanceError.html#method.into_either_with)

#### fn [into\_either\_with](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)
<F>(self, into\_left: F) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

where F: [FnOnce](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html "trait core::ops::function::FnOnce")
(&Self) -> [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
,

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left(&self)` returns `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/execution/struct.BitmexExecutionClient.html#method.into_either)

#### fn [into\_either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)
(self, into\_left: [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left` is `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)

[Source](https://docs.rs/either/1/src/either/into_either.rs.html#55-57)

#### fn [into\_either\_with](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)
<F>(self, into\_left: F) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

where F: [FnOnce](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html "trait core::ops::function::FnOnce")
(&Self) -> [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
,

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left(&self)` returns `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)

# nautilus_bybit - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_bybit](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/index.html#)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_bybit Copy item path
====================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/src/nautilus_bybit/lib.rs.html#16-65)

Expand description

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 adapter for the [Bybit](https://www.bybit.com/)
 cryptocurrency exchange.

The `nautilus-bybit` crate provides client bindings (HTTP & WebSocket), data models, and helper utilities that wrap the official **Bybit v5 API**.

The official Bybit API reference can be found at [https://bybit-exchange.github.io/docs/v5/intro](https://bybit-exchange.github.io/docs/v5/intro)
. All public links inside this crate reference the English version of the documentation.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/index.html#platform)
Platform
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

NautilusTrader’s design, architecture, and implementation philosophy prioritizes software correctness and safety at the highest level, with the aim of supporting mission-critical trading system backtesting and live deployment workloads.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case (Rust-only builds vs. Python bindings through PyO3).

*   `python`: Enables Python bindings via [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds as a Python extension module (used together with `python`).

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/index.html#documentation)
Documentation
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

See [https://docs.rs/nautilus-bybit](https://docs.rs/nautilus-bybit)
 for the latest API documentation.

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/index.html#modules)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[common](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/common/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::common")

Shared primitives and utilities for the Bybit adapter.

[config](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/config/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::config")

Configuration structures for the Bybit adapter.

[data](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/data/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::data")

Data client implementation for the Bybit adapter.

[error](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/error/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::error")

Unified error handling for the Bybit adapter.

[execution](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/execution/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::execution")

Execution client implementation for the Bybit adapter.

[http](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/http/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::http")

HTTP client bindings for the Bybit adapter.

[python](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/python/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::python")

Python bindings from `pyo3`.

[websocket](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_bybit/websocket/index.html "mod nautilus_bybit::websocket")

WebSocket client bindings for the Bybit adapter.

---

Expand description

Represents the configuration for one specific backtest run. This includes a backtest engine with its actors and strategies, with the external inputs of venues and data.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_bitmex/data/struct.BitmexDataClient.html#method.into_either)

#### fn [into\_either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)
(self, into\_left: [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left` is `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)

#### fn [into\_either\_with](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)
<F>(self, into\_left: F) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

where F: [FnOnce](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html "trait core::ops::function::FnOnce")
(&Self) -> [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
,

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left(&self)` returns `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)

Struct BacktestDataClient Copy item path

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_backtest/data_client.rs.html#48-52)

    pub struct BacktestDataClient {
        pub client_id: ClientId,
        pub venue: Venue,
        /* private fields */
    }

Expand description

Data client implementation for backtesting market data operations.

The `BacktestDataClient` provides a data client interface specifically designed for backtesting environments. It handles market data subscriptions and requests during backtesting, coordinating with the backtesting engine to provide historical data replay functionality.

Fields[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/data_client/struct.BacktestDataClient.html#fields)

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_backtest/data_client/struct.BacktestDataClient.html#method.into_either)

#### fn [into\_either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)
(self, into\_left: [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left` is `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either)

#### fn [into\_either\_with](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)
<F>(self, into\_left: F) -> [Either](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
<Self, Self>

where F: [FnOnce](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html "trait core::ops::function::FnOnce")
(&Self) -> [bool](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.bool.html)
,

Converts `self` into a [`Left`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Left "variant either::Either::Left")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 if `into_left(&self)` returns `true`. Converts `self` into a [`Right`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html#variant.Right "variant either::Either::Right")
 variant of [`Either<Self, Self>`](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/enum.Either.html "enum either::Either")
 otherwise. [Read more](https://docs.rs/either/1/either/into_either/trait.IntoEither.html#method.into_either_with)

# PortfolioAnalyzer in nautilus_analysis::analyzer - Rust

[PortfolioAnalyzer](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/struct.PortfolioAnalyzer.html#)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[nautilus\_analysis](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/index.html)
::[analyzer](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/index.html)

Struct PortfolioAnalyzer Copy item path
=======================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_analysis/analyzer.rs.html#54-61)

    #[repr(C)]pub struct PortfolioAnalyzer {
        pub statistics: AHashMap<String, Statistic>,
        pub account_balances_starting: AHashMap<Currency, Money>,
        pub account_balances: AHashMap<Currency, Money>,
        pub positions: Vec<Position>,
        pub realized_pnls: AHashMap<Currency, Vec<(PositionId, f64)>>,
        pub returns: Returns,
    }

Expand description

Analyzes portfolio performance and calculates various statistics.

The `PortfolioAnalyzer` tracks account balances, positions, and realized PnLs to provide comprehensive portfolio analysis including returns, PnL calculations, and customizable statistics.

Fields[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/struct.PortfolioAnalyzer.html#fields)

( &mut self, account: &dyn Account, positions: &\[Position\], )

( &self, currency: [Option](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/option/enum.Option.html "enum core::option::Option")

<[Vec](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/alloc/vec/struct.Vec.html "struct alloc::vec::Vec")
<(PositionId, [f64](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.f64.html)
)>>

Retrieves realized PnLs for a specific currency.

Returns `None` if no PnLs exist, or if multiple currencies exist without an explicit currency specified.

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_analysis/analyzer.rs.html#222-260)

#### pub fn [total\_pnl](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/struct.PortfolioAnalyzer.html#method.total_pnl)
( &self, currency: [Option](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/option/enum.Option.html "enum core::option::Option")
<&Currency>, unrealized\_pnl: [Option](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/option/enum.Option.html "enum core::option::Option")
<&Money>, ) -> [Result](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/core/result/enum.Result.html "enum core::result::Result")
<[f64](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.f64.html)
, &'static [str](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.str.html)
\>

Calculates total PnL including unrealized PnL if provided.

##### [§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/struct.PortfolioAnalyzer.html#panics)
Panics

This function does not panic. The internal `expect` is guarded by a length check ensuring at least one currency exists.

##### [§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/struct.PortfolioAnalyzer.html#errors)
Errors

Returns an error if:

*   No currency is specified in a multi-currency portfolio.
*   The specified currency is not found in account balances.
*   The unrealized PnL currency does not match the specified currency.

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/src/nautilus_analysis/analyzer.rs.html#275-321)

This function does not panic. The internal `expect` is guarded by a length check ensuring at least one currency exists.

Returns an error if:

*   No currency is specified in a multi-currency portfolio.

##### [§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-latest/nautilus_analysis/analyzer/struct.PortfolioAnalyzer.html#errors-2)
Errors

Returns an error if PnL calculations fail, for example due to:

*   No currency specified for a multi-currency portfolio.
*   Unrealized PnL currency not matching the specified currency.
*   Specified currency not found in account balances.

# nautilus_blockchain - Rust

[Crate nautilus\_blockchain](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Crate nautilus\_blockchain Copy item path
=========================================

[Source](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/src/nautilus_blockchain/lib.rs.html#16-80)

Expand description

Blockchain data adapter for [NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
.

The `nautilus-blockchain` crate provides a high-performance, universal, extensible adapter for ingesting DeFi data from decentralized exchanges (DEXs), liquidity pools, and on-chain events. It enables you to power analytics pipelines and trading strategies with real-time and historical on-chain data.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#platform)
Platform
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[NautilusTrader](http://nautilustrader.io/)
 is an open-source, high-performance, production-grade algorithmic trading platform, providing quantitative traders with the ability to backtest portfolios of automated trading strategies on historical data with an event-driven engine, and also deploy those same strategies live, with no code changes.

[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#feature-flags)
Feature Flags
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This crate provides feature flags to control source code inclusion during compilation, depending on the intended use case, i.e. whether to provide Python bindings for the [nautilus\_trader](https://pypi.org/project/nautilus_trader)
 Python package, or as part of a Rust only build.

*   `hypersync`: Enables the [HyperSync](https://envio.dev/#hypersync)
     client integration.
*   `python`: Enables Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
    .
*   `extension-module`: Builds as a Python extension module (used with `python`).
*   `turmoil`: Enables deterministic network simulation testing with [turmoil](https://github.com/tokio-rs/turmoil)
    .

Modules[§](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/index.html#modules)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[cache](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/cache/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::cache")

Caching layer for blockchain entities and domain objects.

[config](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/config/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::config")

[constants](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/constants/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::constants")

[contracts](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/contracts/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::contracts")

Smart contract interfaces and utilities for blockchain interactions.

[data](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/data/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::data")

[decode](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/decode/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::decode")

[events](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/events/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::events")

Blockchain event data structures and parsers.

[exchanges](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/exchanges/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::exchanges")

[execution](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/execution/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::execution")

[factories](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/factories/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::factories")

Factory for creating blockchain data clients.

[hypersync](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/hypersync/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::hypersync")

HyperSync client integration for efficient blockchain data indexing.

[math](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/math/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::math")

Mathematical utilities for blockchain value conversion.

[python](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/python/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::python")

Python bindings from [PyO3](https://pyo3.rs/)
.

[rpc](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/rpc/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::rpc")

RPC client implementations for blockchain network communication.

[services](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/core-nightly/nautilus_blockchain/services/index.html "mod nautilus_blockchain::services")

# Python | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: nightly

On this page

The [Python](https://www.python.org/)
 programming language is used for the majority of user-facing code in NautilusTrader. Python provides a rich ecosystem of libraries and frameworks, making it ideal for strategy development, data analysis, and system integration.

Code style[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#code-style "Direct link to Code style")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### PEP-8[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#pep-8 "Direct link to PEP-8")

The codebase generally follows the PEP-8 style guide. One notable departure is that Python truthiness is not always taken advantage of to check if an argument is `None` for everything other than collections.

As per the [Google Python Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html)
, it's discouraged to use truthiness to check if an argument is/is not `None`, when there is a chance an unexpected object could be passed into the function or method which will yield an unexpected truthiness evaluation (which could result in a logical error type bug).

_"Always use if foo is None: (or is not None) to check for a None value. E.g., when testing whether a variable or argument that defaults to None was set to some other value. The other value might be a value that's false in a boolean context!"_

note

Use truthiness to check for empty collections (e.g., `if not my_list:`) rather than comparing explicitly to `None` or empty.

We welcome all feedback on where the codebase departs from PEP-8 for no apparent reason.

### Type hints[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#type-hints "Direct link to Type hints")

All function and method signatures _must_ include comprehensive type annotations:

    def __init__(self, config: EMACrossConfig) -> None:def on_bar(self, bar: Bar) -> None:def on_save(self) -> dict[str, bytes]:def on_load(self, state: dict[str, bytes]) -> None:

**Union syntax**: Use PEP 604 union syntax for optional types:

    # Preferreddef get_instrument(self, id: InstrumentId) -> Instrument | None:# Avoiddef get_instrument(self, id: InstrumentId) -> Optional[Instrument]:

**Generic types**: Use `TypeVar` for reusable components:

    T = TypeVar("T")class ThrottledEnqueuer(Generic[T]):

### Docstrings[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#docstrings "Direct link to Docstrings")

The [NumPy docstring spec](https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html)
 is used throughout the codebase. This needs to be adhered to consistently to ensure the docs build correctly.

**Python** docstrings should be written in the **imperative mood** – e.g. _"Return a cached client."_

This convention aligns with the prevailing style of the Python ecosystem and makes generated documentation feel natural to end-users.

#### Private methods[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#private-methods "Direct link to Private methods")

Do not add docstrings to private methods (prefixed with `_`):

*   Docstrings generate public-facing API documentation.
*   Docstrings on private methods incorrectly imply they are part of the public API.
*   Private methods are implementation details not intended for end-users.

Exceptions where docstrings are acceptable:

*   Very complex methods with non-trivial logic, multiple steps, or important edge cases.
*   Methods requiring detailed parameter or return value documentation due to complexity.

When a private method needs context (such as a tricky precondition or side effect), prefer a short inline comment (`#`) near the relevant logic rather than a docstring.

### Test naming[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#test-naming "Direct link to Test naming")

Descriptive names explaining the scenario:

    def test_currency_with_negative_precision_raises_overflow_error(self):def test_sma_with_no_inputs_returns_zero_count(self):def test_sma_with_single_input_returns_expected_value(self):

### Ruff[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#ruff "Direct link to Ruff")

[ruff](https://astral.sh/ruff)
 is utilized to lint the codebase. Ruff rules can be found in the top-level `pyproject.toml`, with ignore justifications typically commented.

Cython (legacy)[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#cython-legacy "Direct link to Cython (legacy)")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Deprecation notice

Cython is being phased out in favor of Rust implementations. New code should use Rust. This section documents legacy Cython code only.

For legacy `.pyx` and `.pxd` files, ensure that all functions and methods returning `void` or a primitive C type (such as `bint`, `int`, `double`) include the `except *` keyword in the signature. This ensures Python exceptions are not ignored.

For more information, see the [Cython docs](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)
.

*   [Code style](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#code-style)
    *   [PEP-8](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#pep-8)
        
    *   [Type hints](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#type-hints)
        
    *   [Docstrings](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#docstrings)
        
    *   [Test naming](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#test-naming)
        
    *   [Ruff](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#ruff)
        
*   [Cython (legacy)](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/python/#cython-legacy)

# Testing | NautilusTrader Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Version: nightly

On this page

Our automated tests serve as executable specifications for the trading platform. A healthy suite documents intended behaviour, gives contributors confidence to refactor, and catches regressions before they reach production. Tests also double as living examples that clarify complex flows and provide rapid CI feedback so issues surface early.

The suite covers these categories:

*   Unit tests
*   Integration tests
*   Acceptance tests
*   Performance tests
*   Memory leak tests

Performance tests help evolve performance-critical components.

Run tests with [pytest](https://docs.pytest.org/)
, our primary test runner. Use parametrized tests and fixtures (e.g., `@pytest.mark.parametrize`) to avoid repetitive code and improve clarity.

Running tests[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#running-tests "Direct link to Running tests")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Python tests[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#python-tests "Direct link to Python tests")

From the repository root:

    make pytest# oruv run --active --no-sync pytest --new-first --failed-first# orpytest

For performance tests:

    make test-performance# oruv run --active --no-sync pytest tests/performance_tests --benchmark-disable-gc --codspeed

The `--benchmark-disable-gc` flag prevents garbage collection from skewing results. Run performance tests in isolation (not with unit tests) to avoid interference.

### Rust tests[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#rust-tests "Direct link to Rust tests")

    make cargo-test# orcargo nextest run --workspace --features "python,ffi,high-precision,defi" --cargo-profile nextest

#### Testing with optional features[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#testing-with-optional-features "Direct link to Testing with optional features")

Use `EXTRA_FEATURES` to include optional features like `capnp` or `hypersync`:

    # Test with capnp featuremake cargo-test EXTRA_FEATURES="capnp"# Test with multiple featuresmake cargo-test EXTRA_FEATURES="capnp hypersync"# Legacy shorthand for hypersyncmake cargo-test HYPERSYNC=true# Test specific crate with featuresmake cargo-test-crate-nautilus-serialization FEATURES="capnp"

### IDE integration[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#ide-integration "Direct link to IDE integration")

*   **PyCharm**: Right-click the tests folder or file → "Run pytest".
*   **VS Code**: Use the Python Test Explorer extension.

Test style[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#test-style "Direct link to Test style")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Name test functions after what they exercise; you do not need to encode the expected assertions in the name.
*   Add docstrings when they clarify setup, scenarios, or expectations.
*   Prefer pytest-style free functions for Python tests instead of test classes with setup methods.
*   **Group assertions** when possible: perform all setup/act steps first, then assert together to avoid the act-assert-act smell.
*   Use `unwrap`, `expect`, or direct `panic!`/`assert` calls inside tests; clarity and conciseness matter more than defensive error handling here.

For Rust-specific test conventions (module structure, `#[rstest]`, parameterization), see the [Rust guide](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/rust#testing-conventions)
.

Waiting for asynchronous effects[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#waiting-for-asynchronous-effects "Direct link to Waiting for asynchronous effects")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When waiting for background work to complete, prefer the polling helpers `await eventually(...)` from `nautilus_trader.test_kit.functions` and `wait_until_async(...)` from `nautilus_common::testing` instead of arbitrary sleeps. They surface failures faster and reduce flakiness in CI because they stop as soon as the condition is satisfied or time out with a useful error.

Mocks[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#mocks "Direct link to Mocks")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Prefer hand-written stubs that return fixed values over mocking frameworks. Use `MagicMock` only when you need to assert call counts/arguments or simulate complex state changes. Avoid mocking the objects you're actually testing.

Code coverage[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#code-coverage "Direct link to Code coverage")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

We generate coverage reports with `coverage` and publish them to [codecov](https://about.codecov.io/)
.

Aim for high coverage without sacrificing appropriate error handling or causing "test induced damage" to the architecture.

Some branches remain untestable without modifying production behaviour. For example, a final condition in a defensive if-else block may only trigger for unexpected values; leave these checks in place so future changes can exercise them if needed.

Design-time exceptions can also be impractical to test, so 100% coverage is not the target.

Excluded code coverage[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#excluded-code-coverage "Direct link to Excluded code coverage")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

We use `pragma: no cover` comments to [exclude code from coverage](https://coverage.readthedocs.io/en/coverage-4.3.3/excluding.html)
 when tests would be redundant. Typical examples include:

*   Asserting an abstract method raises `NotImplementedError` when called.
*   Asserting the final condition check of an if-else block when impossible to test (as above).

Such tests are expensive to maintain because they must track refactors while providing little value. Ensure concrete implementations of abstract methods remain fully covered. Remove `pragma: no cover` when it no longer applies and restrict its use to the cases above.

Debugging Rust tests[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#debugging-rust-tests "Direct link to Debugging Rust tests")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use the default test configuration to debug Rust tests.

To run the full suite with debug symbols for later, run `make cargo-test-debug` instead of `make cargo-test`.

In IntelliJ IDEA, adjust the run configuration for parametrised `#[rstest]` cases so it reads `test --package nautilus-model --lib data::bar::tests::test_get_time_bar_start::case_1` (remove `-- --exact` and append `::case_n` where `n` starts at 1). This workaround matches the behaviour explained [here](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-analyzer/issues/8964#issuecomment-871592851)
.

In VS Code you can pick the specific test case to debug directly.

Python + Rust Mixed Debugging[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#python--rust-mixed-debugging "Direct link to Python + Rust Mixed Debugging")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This workflow lets you debug Python and Rust code simultaneously from a Jupyter notebook inside VS Code.

### Setup[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#setup "Direct link to Setup")

Install these VS Code extensions: Rust Analyzer, CodeLLDB, Python, Jupyter.

### Step 0: Compile `nautilus_trader` with debug symbols[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#step-0-compile-nautilus_trader-with-debug-symbols "Direct link to step-0-compile-nautilus_trader-with-debug-symbols")

    cd nautilus_trader && make build-debug-pyo3

### Step 1: Set up debugging configuration[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#step-1-set-up-debugging-configuration "Direct link to Step 1: Set up debugging configuration")

    from nautilus_trader.test_kit.debug_helpers import setup_debuggingsetup_debugging()

This command creates the required VS Code debugging configurations and starts a `debugpy` server for the Python debugger.

By default `setup_debugging()` expects the `.vscode` folder one level above the `nautilus_trader` root directory. Adjust the target location if your workspace layout differs.

### Step 2: Set breakpoints[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#step-2-set-breakpoints "Direct link to Step 2: Set breakpoints")

*   **Python breakpoints:** Set in VS Code in the Python source files.
*   **Rust breakpoints:** Set in VS Code in the Rust source files.

### Step 3: Start mixed debugging[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#step-3-start-mixed-debugging "Direct link to Step 3: Start mixed debugging")

1.  In VS Code select the **"Debug Jupyter + Rust (Mixed)"** configuration.
2.  Start debugging (F5) or press the green run arrow.
3.  Both Python and Rust debuggers attach to your Jupyter session.

### Step 4: Execute code[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#step-4-execute-code "Direct link to Step 4: Execute code")

Run Jupyter notebook cells that call Rust functions. The debugger stops at breakpoints in both Python and Rust code.

### Available configurations[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#available-configurations "Direct link to Available configurations")

`setup_debugging()` creates these VS Code configurations:

*   **`Debug Jupyter + Rust (Mixed)`** - Mixed debugging for Jupyter notebooks.
*   **`Jupyter Mixed Debugging (Python)`** - Python-only debugging for notebooks.
*   **`Rust Debugger (for Jupyter debugging)`** - Rust-only debugging for notebooks.

### Example[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#example "Direct link to Example")

Open and run the example notebook: `debug_mixed_jupyter.ipynb`.

### Reference[​](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#reference "Direct link to Reference")

*   [PyO3 debugging](https://pyo3.rs/v0.25.1/debugging.html?highlight=deb#debugging-from-jupyter-notebooks)
    

*   [Running tests](https://nautilustrader.io/docs/nightly/developer_guide/testing/#running-tests)

[](https://github.com/nautechsystems/nautilus_trader)
